item.title: Junior Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image "map.jpg" used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license. Found here, the image "Kokerei Zollverein IMGP4891 wp.jpg" by smial is licensed under the Free Art License, which is not compatible with the site's Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 License. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the beginning of this portrays threes see SCPs as fiery hell creatures that hate everything, but it's later revealed its the Foundation's fault. They hate everything because the Foundation literally took away their capacity to love anything at all. It's terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Flue gas. He's explaining how, if the flue gas can't escape, oxygen won't be able to get in (because of positive air-pressure inside the furnaces), leading to the fires going out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the ending section, you said flue gas. Did you mean to say fuel gas, or did you mean to say flue gas?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I've found my new favorite skip. From start to finish, you managed to keep building the tension, and that last memo from Hammond sealed the deal. Hard +1, and this alone establishes you as one of the best authors this site's ever had (as though there was any doubt).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has always been one of my favorites. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit. That was… honestly disturbing.

+1

(Also apparently the emails work with Outlook, I discovered by accident.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for this clarification, I was having trouble understanding exactly what was going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen it in a few of your skips, and I really appreciate your resistance to the dehumanization of D-Class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's how it works:
You magically boil happy memories out of a corpse's brain. You catch those happy memories, mix them with all the other happy memories you took from other corpses (hence 'happy soup'), then turn this happy memory soup into an amnestic.

Here's what happened:
The corpses had all their happy memories magically boiled out of them — leaving nothing but corpses with hateful, violent memories behind. These bad memories caused the corpses to come back to life as hateful, violent monsters.

Here's the point:
West was comfortable with this because he saw D-Class as hateful, violent criminals — and nothing else. In his mind, they gave up their right to be treated with dignity and respect (in life or death). Their "monstrous nature" made them (and their corpses) disposable.

But the problem is that when you reduce a group of people to monsters — and treat them like monsters? — eventually, some of them will become monsters. And that's what 2419-1 instances are: They're monsters. West (unintentionally) finally found a way to turn D-Class into the irredeemable monsters he imagined them to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
think of it as a 'distillation' of memories — of our happiest experiences. All the parts of a human life that bring us comfort when we need it most — that make us gentle and kind. Now, we can combine and homogenize all of these purified distillations together, to create a compound that, when properly processed and combined with targeted hypnotherapy, allows for the removal of precise memories, specific memories. We replace them with this 'happy soup', if you will — and their minds use that soup to build new, pleasant false memories — filling in the gaps.

I must be misinterpreting this. I saw this as Dr. West taking all of their memories away, purifying them so that only the good stuff remains, and putting the memories back. After a couple reads, I realize that the "happy soup" didn't work the way Dr. West intended as the new memories formed are bad ones instead of good ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What are you talking about? Dr. Hammond wasn't told to boil away the bad memories; he was ordered to boil away the good ones. That's how the process works: You boil the good memories, distill them into the amnestic, then throw away/burn what's left.

And what's left eventually turned out to be immortal regenerating murder-zombies full of purified hate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So why did Dr. Hammond boil away the good memories instead the bad ones like he was directed to? To see if he can create savage monsters out of prisoners?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My biggest question is why Dr. Hammond created a regenerating zombie savage by taking away everything that made the D-Class decent when Dr. West was trying to do the opposite. It makes no sense to do so and only made an already bad situation worse.

Neither Dr. Hammond nor Dr. West had any idea that they were creating regenerating zombies. Dr. Hammond suspected something like this might happen (which is why he left), but he didn't create regenerating zombies on purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My biggest question is why Dr. Hammond created a regenerating zombie savage by taking away everything that made the D-Class decent when Dr. West was trying to do the opposite. It makes no sense to do so and only made an already bad situation worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was reading the comments and got sad about the fact that the author wasn't with us anymore, but then I read the last one and realized he just got another account and that made me smile. Superb piece of work, upvoted with all my might.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, wow, thank you! I really appreciate the feedback. <3

One thing I'd like to point out: Something I failed to hammer home in this article is that they aren't actually skeletons; they just look like humans. The picture is a skeleton because when they're in the incinerator, most of their flesh is being burned away.

I've struggled a long time whether or not to adjust the article to clarify this. On one hand, the whole point is that they look like us, but just have had their kindness stripped away — on the other hand, an army of laughing skeletons is simultaneously so terrifying yet so cheesy that I've come to really like this interpretation.

ETA: Also, yes, one of my big regrets with this article is that 'how does the Foundation deal with anomalous waste materials, such as corpses?' is such a fantastic writing prompt, and though I like where I took it, I feel like the answer to this question could be so much bigger (and lead to so many more stories).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, it's gush time. Three things I love about this.

One: I love stories that tug on the loose ends of SCP lore to see where they might lead. Exploring the idea of how the Foundation disposes of potentially anomalous corpses is positively inspired.

Two: The way this is structured is fantastic. Each collapsible peels back another layer, showing a little bit more, causing you to see the SCP in a slightly different light each time. What's more, each layer is interesting in its own right, presenting a new set of mysteries every time. That's a hell of a trick to pull off.

Three: I can forgive a deficit of real-world logic (cement!) as long as it's paid back in good ol' fashion nightmare logic, and this pays back in spades. "If the incinerator shuts down, the skeletons will get out." "We didn't just burn away their flesh, we burned away their humanity." "They lose their good thoughts so you can lose your bad ones." These things are the sort nonsense all the best nightmares are made of.

Gush time over.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP reminds me greatly of SCP-3000. There are a number of shared elements - both involve the removal of memories and the death of higher consciousness, so that the Foundation can get a special compound used in the refinement of amnestics. There are similarities between the "residue" and Y-909 that could be made.

Editing this:

WEST: It turns out that the human brain, ah, the human brain — so long as it's relatively well preserved — leaves behind… there's a certain residue that it leaves behind. Something we can extract, and, ah, refine.

To this:

WEST: It turns out that the human brain, ah, the human brain — so long as it's relatively well preserved — leaves behind… there's a certain residue that it leaves behind. Something we can extract, and, ah, refine.

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does this remind anyone else of that episode of Tales From The Crypt when the mad doctor was intent on finding the 'soul' gland and removing it?
"You don't do this… You're a good person!"

" Not anymore, you took my soul away!"

Also, I loved the shrink basically saying: " I think we're all human beings worthy of some compassion. But not these f***ers. Throw them in the worst part of hell and curse God for not having somewhere worse to put them."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is terrific, Hippo. This doesn't fit into my top 10, but it's definitely in my top 50.

+1

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: I read this post to the tune of "still alive". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can totally understand that, but glad that this is of some help.

I still think the cement way is possible tho, just wall off one section and wait after they finished regenerating and then pour in the cement, maybe :P Unless by regenerating you mean… infinitely growing. (But yeah it's not the point anyways, I just wanted to say the threat isn't super convincing.)

Personally I don't think making them more anomalous than regenerating sadists would be better, because I actually like that part about them just being that. It's sufficient enough, and not too anomalous to make people doubt how would a memory extracting porcedure cause that.

Them crawling out of the fires is actually pretty horrifying, and I feel like a lot my doubting comes from the repeated adressing, which gives me time and motive to question the reality of this… Otherwise I really wouldn't think all this deep into it. I actually personally think maybe just editing the last log would help, or even just get rid of the laughter reference before, and replace it with vague suggestions that those may be laughters. But then again, just my opinions :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's fine, and yes, a lot of the stuff you said makes perfect sense! There's a lot of things in this skip I would like to address; I sometimes have a tendency to to rush my ideas out based on a fear that if I don't, I won't ever actually complete them. In some ways, this skip is that.

Detailed Response
– hide block

That being said, the things I would have to change (in particular, to address some of the stuff you're bringing up — but also changes that I've thought about myself) would involve almost a complete rewrite — which, given people's response, leaves me in a weird position. I don't want to tell people they need to re-evaluate it, but I also don't want to change it so much that someone might regret their upvote and never get around to adjusting it. And deleting/reposting seems unfair to people who really like the original skip.

Something I'm trying to move toward with these skips is learning to accept that they're not going to be perfect — and so long as people get something out of them, that's okay. That probably sounds like an excuse to just give up on fixing this :P (and maybe it is!).

But it's also worth noting that even if I don't end up rewriting this, feedback like you're giving is still extremely helpful for me. It helps me understand how others digest my stories, which — in turn — helps me develop better techniques for the next one.

So, what I'm saying is — even if I don't end up acting on the stuff you're bringing up, it's still very useful for me to hear it, and I'm immensely appreciative of the feedback!

(Sidenote: I will say that the reason they don't just turn the heaters down and fill them gradually with cement is because that would almost certainly not work: If flesh is anomalously regenerating, it must exert pressure as it expands; the pressure of 1000+ expanding human bodies would be more than sufficient to crack just about any material you tried to embed them in. You can get away with doing it to one body, maybe, but a thousand? Probably not! But, I mean, your point is still valid — the Foundation could just enclose the heater in a gigantic macro-structure, then seal the entrance. Heck, just enclose it in an unclimbable wall — problem solved! :P There are a lot of ways the Foundation could mitigate this threat, and a lot of missed opportunities in just making them ultra-violent regenerating super-sadists)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably just me, but half way through the skip I realized what's going on, and from there it's okay, but the end note just completely breaks it for me, especially the last line.

Title aside, the fact that they laugh is already mentioned, even very early on in the story. The last line therefore was not much of a reveal, but a forced dramatic moment. In fact, the rest of the last note doesn't do much as well, aside from exposition and repeating what we already know. Aside from that, why would an operator knows about the memory extraction? It is awkward to see the note went from complaining that 2419 would break out to talking about how cruel and pissed they are, and that just make the exposition factor much more glaring.

And in fact, the more the "they're going to break out" is highlighted, the more they talk about the horrible things they did to the D-Classes, the less the skip works for me, and it became harder to believe in. It was actually quite nice back in the log where the procedure oft extracting happy memories is explained, but somehow three logs later I'm less convinced than I started out to be.

The threat at the end is just… someone saying there is a threat, and as I think about it, the notion becomes ridiculous because if those former D-Classes just regenerates, why would Foundation keep the burner going and waster resources on something that would keep regenerate? It's not 682's case where it can adapt, these are regenerating corpses, and I imagine halfway down the line someone is going to decide to graudally turn down the fire and just put cement or some other kind of stuff in bit by bit, because it's not only more practial and cheaper. The incident log where the one instance killed the doctor does not help, because the thing doesn't show any remarkable power, and feels like the doctor is just being kind of incompetent and careless. Even if he wants to show care to the instance, it just feels like he should know better.

So at the end, the threat just isn't super real, and I would even question the direction of making them a big threat? Why not let them just be former humans, extracted last bit of good memories, thrown down into the pit by the Foundation, now still helplessly burnt by the Foundation? Wouldn't that be more irony of a situation? I guess this bit is just my personal preference, but there could also be better and more convining ways to set up the threat as well.

I actually liked the skip during certain parts, but yeah, -1 from me. I hoped the things I said makes sense and would help, but maybe it's just me since it's mostly positive feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey — thanks for the response! Admittedly, I haven't tried anything large-scale (even on my author's page, I think my longest skip is probably the first Murphy Law or Video Game Violence); writing stamina is a bit of a problem for me. I've been trying to work on some more expansive things on the side, though.

Re: murderous skeletons — a thing I think I did a poor job getting across is that these things aren't actually skeletal; that one picture looks like a skeleton because the instance has been burned down to one. When they're out of the incinerator (and have a chance to regenerate), they look like ordinary people.

Also, yeah — the regeneration is kind of arbitrary; it's basically a result of Dr. West's (largely undescribed) process which ended up making corpses anomalous over time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certainly a fascinating idea, and a wonderful build of tension throughout the piece (the idea of 10,000 regenerating murderous skeletons tends to be a bit worrisome).

As a lot of people have mentioned, you seem to be putting out work at a remarkable rate, yet this skip appears remarkably thought-out (except for the bit about the entities being able to regenerate, which appears to be a random addition unless I missed something). In any case I've seen a few of your pieces and while they are all brilliantly terrifying and cool, I'd have to say I'd love to see what would happen if you took a bit longer to make some more long-form skips (a la SCP-610). Not saying you should slow down or change your style, I'm just curious to find out what would happen if you wrote something on a larger scale.

TL:DR: Love your work, love this skip, imo you could do some cool work in a long-form piece.

IMMEDIATE EDIT: I never realized you had an author page, and am only just now discovering your previous works, which no doubt contain many skips of greater length. I feel stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's something about this skip and oddly appropriate songs. I first read this one a few days ago, and then today I heard The Fire by Griffinilla. It feels like it fits so well…

+ Lyrics
- Lyrics

Deep in the woods, there was a Fire
That burned with an evil desire
Desire to feed on the pain
A flame that would never retire
A pyre that would drive you insane
And it said

Why don't you cast yourself on me?
I promise you won't feel a thing
There's golden treasures underneath, you'll see!
Now please, give yourself to me!

Deep in the woods, the Fire grew higher
The flesh would dissolve and expire
But the bones and the ashes remained
They whistled and snapped like a choir
A chorus that cried out in vain
And it said

Why don't you cast yourself on me?
I promise you won't feel a thing
There's golden treasures underneath, you'll see!
Now please, give yourself to me!

Deep in the woods, there's still the Fire
That will never grow weary or tired
And draws closer and closer each day
And in bed, you start to perspire
Pray you don't hear that refrain

give yourself to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I think what I like most about this is that you took a really tired old concept (Zombies that fight good) and made it interesting, creepy, and emotional. Great writing, great work all around.

item.title: So /that's/ why 3667-2 crosstesting's a bad idea...
item.content:encoded: I loved this, from start to finish: this feels like a beautiful sequel to all the skips you mentioned above (plus I picked up some flavours of SCP-1730, although that may just be me). I'm honoured that this is (at least partially) written in response to one of my own articles, and I think the fact that this is on trajectory to surpass SCP-3667 says a lot. Congratulations. :)

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: Hard +1. Manages to be menacing without being a "If ur reading this it'll get yoooouuuu", and the communications from different perspectives build tension very well. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think "sociopathy" is a more accurate term for what you're describing*, though an older professional might still use "psychopathy" out of habit.

Edit: I meant, what Hippo is describing in the article.

Sociopathy is a complex and uniquely human set of.behaviors that shouldn't really be applied to skeleton monsters, which is exactly the point the doctor is making in the line, so it read fine to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you invent this greate concept, wrote the whole greate story, made amazing twists and emotional punches, All that and a little bit more, just for we can one more time hear "They're dead, Jim." from an old doctor?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Also, it was super-hard to choose between your song and the one I used in the author's post (I went with the latter just, outside of a line about 'mechanical parts', the lyrics line up with it almost too perfectly).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Life, uh, finds a way.

This was great. I think the conclusion was actually the weakest part of this for me, which is a good thing because I liked it. The first interview is satisfyingly simple, in a way I haven't much of; it harks back to Disgusting and the other basic stuff and here it's very effective at eliciting that repulsive, gut reaction. The shrink's report was my favorite bit, even though it's essentially a load of exposition it's very well written and it's also made leagues more horrible by the climax. Dr. West played by the Artist formerly known as Jeff Goldblum is a despicable character snd I appreciste the venom with which he constructs his letter to Dr. Hammond - he averts that cold, calculating scientist trope nicely. I also liked the lore bits - even if they ended up resulting in an enromous death army of immortal child murderers being unleashed upon the humsn race, it's just an interesting look at Foundation policy in regards to amnestics' nature and their usage at the lower levels of the bureaucracy.

+1

e: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q6-ZGAGcJrk

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Moklin: A lot of what you're saying is confusing to me; you interpret a lot of things much differently than I intended them to be taken. For example, they know the instance is John simply because someone recognized him (visibly, it's still John; I wanted to subtly imply that Warren might have even known him when he was still D-Class); the guard doesn't try to run, he just shouts an expletive, stumbles back in shock, un-holsters his gun, and then proceeds to shoot the instance — etc.

I don't want to go down and address your list point by point — I don't feel like this is the place for it, and how you interpreted it is how you interpreted it. I'll keep your interpretation in mind as one way some of my stuff might be taken in the future. For example, the mention of your preference in audio logs for more straightforward descriptors regarding what's going on is particularly helpful.

Thank you for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding
– hide block

The premise is played too straight for my liking, and there's a lot of narm. "/2419/interviews/109.log" in particular annoys me. Furthermore, the inspiration you drew from 1730 and 3000 are blindingly obvious, to the point where I have to say that this is derivative. (I didn't assume they're called laughing men because it shows up in one "interview" log. Chekov's gun told me that it would be really important later on, which spoiled it.)

Line-by-line

http://scp-wiki.wdfiles.com/local--files/scp-2419/instance.jpg

^ This looks like it was taken straight from a movie, which immediately broke my suspension of disbelief.

WARREN:

…Talks like he's either insane or a pre-school teacher. Or both. (Seriously, just read through the interview again but read Warren's lines in baby-talk, it reads way more fluidly than it should.)

(Soft ripping.)

This is a problem that I have with a lot of your stuff but couldn't properly address because I liked the rest of the articles.
Your descriptors aren't clinical. (This was also a problem with Site-5)
Instead of simply saying that the damn thing is picking at its arm, you go for the more implicit direction. While I understand the reasoning, it's more geared towards prose than scientific documentation.

We know you can speak. We've recorded you laughing.

…That doesn't mean they can talk. Laughing is a universal human expression, language isn't. E.G. a 2-month-old baby can laugh, smile, and cry, but you'd be crazy for expecting it to say anything other than variations of "goo-goo-ga-ga".

WARREN: Why won't you speak to us?

(Soft ripping continues.)

WARREN: Is there anything we can do to change your mind?

(Soft ripping continues.)

WARREN: Look… I brought you something.

(Soft ripping stops.)

WARREN: It's a picture. Of your family. Back before — back when your name was John. Back before you were D-Class. Do you remember…?

(Silence.)

WARREN: You had a wife. Two sons. A little baby girl.

(Silence.)

WARREN: (softly) Do you remember, John?

Requesting whatever the hell Warren's smoking. Also, how the hell can they accurately tell the genetic makeup of a regenerator from the next, especially when there's 10,000 of them? Or are all D-Class just called John now?

(Shattering glass.)

GUARD: Shit! He's—

I think you'd be hard-pressed to find a paramilitary guard who's first instinct is "flight", or "abandon all hope". The absolute first-response when dealing with an immortal killing-machine that doesn't adapt to what you throw at it is to shoot it.

They are well beyond any definition of psychopathy

No they aren't. Unless you count "no higher brain functions" as beyond any definition of psychopathy. Furthermore, from what I understand, psychopathy isn't even a legitimately recognized condition, with the defining characteristics having their own definitions and labels.

The memory juice extraction bit is basically lifted from 3000 without alteration, and the presentation of it gives away the goose too quickly.


So yeah, those are my thoughts. Didn't like it for the reasons specified, downvoted. Apologies if this came off as mean.

EDIT: Talked this over with hippo over IRC, turns out a lot of the problems I have are based off of poor communication on either end. Neutral-voting for now, and hoping to find this again some day when the amnestic concept has been made unique enough to not make me think of SCP-3000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably, but I just liked the name so much I couldn't help myself. >_>

(Also I'm hoping that readers presume it's called 'the laughing men' because of the interview log)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great skip! But I think the title "The Laughing Men" gives away the last twist.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just doesn't work for me. All the emotional beats pass by with no effect, the characters are two-dimensional, and the themes are beat over my head. I can't say that I found the article interesting or engaging.

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
So, I made a major change to the final memo from Dr. Hammond (primarily the central part) because I felt like there's a theme I wasn't making clear enough — it's partly why I wrote this skip, and I thought it was super-important to elaborate on. Hopefully, nobody who's voted so far will mind the change; I don't think it's a big one, although it does make something initially implied by the skip far more overt. Included here, in case you don't want to go look at the skip to read it:

► ACCESS SCP:/2419/memos/068.log
▼ Close File

MEMO

DATE: 1974/09/13
FROM: Dr. Hammond
TO: Dr. Kerringer

You've always been very kind. Thank you for that.

Yesterday, I processed the body of D-263175. He was sentenced to death for the murder of his wife. I boiled away the six hours he spent holding his sister's hand as she lay dying in a hospital bed. I boiled away the swell of pride he felt when his son took his first few steps. I boiled away all the kindness his mother ever showed him — until all that remained was her cruelty and abuse.

I took away every moment of joy — and left behind a life of uninterrupted misery, pain, and rage.

Do you know why the Foundation takes D-Class personnel from criminal populations, Shaun? It's because nobody stops us. Nobody cares. A prison is where you put unimportant people. It's where you shave down whatever is left of their humanity until the monster you imagined them to be is all that remains. But no matter how hard we tried, there was always a sliver of decency left behind — some piece of them we couldn't quite reach.

Until now. We've done it. In death, D-263175 is finally the monster people imagined. I've burned away every last trace of his humanity. Now, I'm going to send his corpse to Unit C and burn away the rest.

I pray to God that what's left will burn.

I can't stay. I'm sorry. I recommend you get out while you still can.

-Dr. Hammond

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone whose first name is Bryan you freaked me the fuck out with that last email so just take the +1 and leave me alone you scary man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every form compliment has been said above, so just take my upvote already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that was pleasantly unpleasant. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm starting to think it's impossible for you to write anything I don't enjoy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually wrote this one in response to having read SCP-3667, which I really liked — also, ty!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks! The presenter's stammer/some of the side bits in that log were an experiment to see how well they read (for credibility purposes); I'm glad it seems to work well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best I've read in a very long time

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. I feel like it compliments 3667, which just so happens to be one of my favorite series IV articles. Good job!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do you pump out so many quality articles in such a short amount of time?

Fantastic. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely disgusting, in every sense of the word. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The speed at which you work is rather impressive.

Sufficiently dark, supremely fucked-up, and certainly disturbing. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just an all around solid SCP, and a great base concept. You're on fire Hippo, and killing it right now. Heavy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked every single thing about the whole concept and mood of this, but somehow the detail that's striking me the most is the presenter's dialogue during the slideshow and how well done his stammer was.

This was metal as hell, every part of it separately was something I liked, and those parts were really well put together. I have no idea how you're churning out such exceptionally solid articles at the rate you are, but I'm glad you're doing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops — thanks! Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dig this on numerous levels. +1

One possible fix to make: Double-check the signature on the memo in the third collapsible(► ACCESS SCP:/2419/memos/221.log)

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
I HOPE YOU DIE IN A FIRE
